text: The surgeon flips the right colon medially to expose the duodenum, revealing adhesions or LADS bands connecting the duodenum to the retroperitoneum and colon. Interloop adhesions causing kinking of the bowel are visible and are divided using a combination of blunt and electrocautery dissection.
The surgeon identifies adhesions between the duodenum and the retroperitoneum, with the duodenum overlying the inferior vena cava (IVC). These adhesions are carefully dissected away.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the right colon by opening the gastrocolic ligament at the mid-transverse colon and dividing it towards the hepatic flexure. The right colon is flipped medially, exposing the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. Adhesions between the duodenum and mesentery are dissected using blunt and electrocautery techniques. A fatty band adhered to the terminal ileum overlying the duodenum is identified and divided to relieve obstruction.

The surgeon runs the small bowel proximally towards the ligament of Treitz, encountering adhesions from the bowel to the root of the mesentery. These adhesions are carefully divided using electrocautery.

After taking down the adhesions, the surgeon broadens the mesentery, a crucial step in the Ladd's procedure. The staple line from the appendectomy is visible, aiding in identifying the colon and small bowel. The small bowel is positioned on the right and the colon in the left upper quadrant to ensure proper mesenteric broadening.
